name: Gravel route of the walled cities
name_original: Ruta gravel de las ciudades amuralladas
description:
In the depths of Castilla y León, in the province of Salamanca, there are two great walled treasures with war heritage that are currently open-air museums of Castilian history. These are Ciudad Rodrigo, a medieval jewel, and the Real Fuerte de la Concepción, a 17th century stronghold.

The route between one and the other runs through the steppes and pastures of Salamanca and takes you to small hidden towns where time seems to have stopped. Likewise, you come across the Águeda River several times, which is home to the archaeological site of Siega Verde, where you can see rock carvings on the stones.

The Route is circular from Ciudad Rodrigo, where you can arrive by train or private vehicle. The route does not pose any technical difficulties, so you can use a gravel bike with high-rolling tires. Even if you pass through various towns, it is recommended that you carry enough water and food for the entire day. You can also choose to divide the tour in two and stay in one of the passing towns. The dry days of spring and autumn are ideal for discovering the walled cities.
region_intro: Gravel biking around Ciudad Rodrigo features a landscape characterized by rolling hills, historic walled cities, and views towards the Sierra de Francia. The terrain includes a mix of unpaved roads and paths, offering varied surfaces for gravel cyclists. Routes often traverse agricultural areas and natural viewpoints, providing diverse riding experiences. The region's topography includes moderate elevation changes, suitable for different skill levels. Best gravel bike trails around Ciudad Rodrigo The most popular gravel bike trail is Gravel route of the walled cities, a 54.6 miles (87.9 km) trail that takes 7 hours 51 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the historic defensive structures of the region. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Collada de la Genera – Views of the Sierra de Francia loop from La Suente, a difficult 53.9 miles (86.7 km) path. This route offers significant elevation gain and panoramic views of the Sierra de Francia. Local gravel bikers also love the Plaza Mayor Ciudad Rodrigo – Cathedral of Santa María loop from Ciudad Rodrigo, a 31.9 miles (51.5 km) trail leading through the historic center and surrounding areas, often completed in about 4 hours 35 minutes. Gravel biking around Ciudad Rodrigo is defined by historic landmarks, rolling terrain, and scenic viewpoints. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Ciudad Rodrigo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 20 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Ciudad Rodrigo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What are the general difficulty levels for gravel biking around Ciudad Rodrigo?
A: The gravel bike trails around Ciudad Rodrigo offer a range of difficulty levels, from easy to difficult. You'll find routes with moderate elevation changes suitable for various skill sets. For instance, an easy option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23741811" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dios le Guarde loop from Dios le Guarde&lt;/a&gt;, which is just under 7 km.

Q: Are there any family-friendly gravel biking routes in the area?
A: Yes, for families or those looking for a relaxed ride, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23741811" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dios le Guarde loop from Dios le Guarde&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent easy option. It's a shorter route, making it suitable for a leisurely outing with less demanding terrain.

Q: What kind of distances can I expect on gravel bike routes near Ciudad Rodrigo?
A: Gravel routes in the region vary significantly in length. You can find shorter loops like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23741811" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dios le Guarde loop from Dios le Guarde&lt;/a&gt; at around 6.7 km, or more challenging, longer rides such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/32135695" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cachón del Camaces Viewpoint – Puente de la Molinera loop from Saldeana&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 65 km.

Q: Are there any circular gravel bike routes available?
A: Many of the gravel routes around Ciudad Rodrigo are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38152302" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tamames Tower – La Sagrada loop from Tamames&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 30 km ride, and the easy &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23741811" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dios le Guarde loop from Dios le Guarde&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some notable viewpoints or scenic spots along the gravel trails?
A: The region is known for its scenic beauty. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/32135695" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cachón del Camaces Viewpoint – Puente de la Molinera loop from Saldeana&lt;/a&gt; offer specific viewpoints. Generally, you can expect panoramic views towards the Sierra de Francia and across agricultural landscapes, especially on routes with higher elevation gain.

Q: What historical landmarks can I see while gravel biking around Ciudad Rodrigo?
A: Ciudad Rodrigo is rich in history, and many routes pass by significant landmarks. You can explore sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4527256" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Enrique II of Trastámara&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3984587" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor Ciudad Rodrigo&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4527228" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cathedral of Santa María&lt;/a&gt;. The region's historic walled cities and defensive structures are a common feature.

Q: What is the best time of year for gravel biking in Ciudad Rodrigo?
A: The best time for gravel biking in Ciudad Rodrigo is typically during spring and autumn when the weather is milder and more predictable. Summers can be hot, and winters might bring cooler temperatures, though the region generally experiences a Mediterranean climate with mild winters.

Q: Are there any moderate gravel routes for intermediate riders?
A: Yes, intermediate riders can find suitable routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38152302" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tamames Tower – La Sagrada loop from Tamames&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 30 km route that offers a good balance of distance and elevation without being overly strenuous, making it a great option for those looking to step up from easy trails.

Q: What should I bring for a gravel bike ride in Ciudad Rodrigo?
A: Given the varied terrain and potential for longer rides, it's advisable to bring plenty of water, snacks, a repair kit, and appropriate sun protection. A helmet is essential, and layers of clothing are recommended to adapt to changing temperatures, especially if riding in spring or autumn.

Q: What do other gravel bikers enjoy most about the routes in Ciudad Rodrigo?
A: The komoot community highly rates the gravel biking experience in Ciudad Rodrigo, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse terrain, the blend of historic landmarks with natural landscapes, and the panoramic views, particularly towards the Sierra de Francia.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the gravel trails?
A: While specific cafes directly on every trail might be limited, many routes pass through or near small towns and villages where you can find local establishments for refreshments. Planning your route to include stops in places like Ciudad Rodrigo itself or Tamames can ensure you have access to amenities.

Q: Are there any permits required for gravel biking on these trails?
A: Generally, no specific permits are required for gravel biking on the public trails and unpaved roads around Ciudad Rodrigo. However, always respect private property and any local signage regarding access or protected areas.
tips: The Plaza Mayor is the center of the city. All activity has always developed around it, in it you can still enjoy interesting buildings.

The Puerta del Alcázar is one of the ancient access gates to the walled enclosure of Ciudad Rodrigo. This gate, along with others such as the Puerta del Sol and the Puerta de Santiago, was part of the city's defensive system.

Classic Salamancan town bordering Portugal. It has just 280 inhabitants, but its abandoned houses tell stories of more prosperous times. It is surrounded by the grain fields and farms that are the main engine of its current economy. There are a couple of bars ideal for a stop on the way.

The municipality of La Alameda de Gardón is located in an area dominated by small depressions caused by the banks, which can reach a 100 meter drop. The urban core is located on the slopes caused by one of these depressions.

The refurbishment and reconversion project of this 17th-century fortress won, in 2014, the prize for the best restoration of a military building in Spain. What are now luxury rooms on the Spanish-Portuguese border could house 1,500 infantrymen and 200 horses at his creation.

The fort had two phases. The first, in the 17th century, when after the uprising in Lisbon that deposed Philip of Habsburg, it was built to serve as a base for an army that would recover Portugal. The Second, in the 18th century, when it was rebuilt to oppose the Portuguese stronghold of Almeida. During the war of independence, it was partially blown up and, for much of the 19th century, the surrounding inhabitants took advantage of it as a quarry until its sale to the private sector gave rise to the current construction.

The zoomorphic carvings of Siega Verde occupy more than a kilometer of stones on the banks of the Águeda and are the most important open-air site on the Iberian Peninsula. They are part of the Vale de Coa complex, in Portugal, which was declared a World Heritage Site by UNESCO.

Horses, bears, goats and other species extinct on the peninsula such as the woolly rhinoceros, reindeer and bison are represented in the carvings. The site is fenced for its preservation, but an interpretation room has been built that details the life of the inhabitants of the area in the Palaeolithic.
